claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is substantially artifactual: word-final glyphs drawn from {y,n,l,r,o} function as inflectional suffixes, and stripping the final glyph of every word reduces unique word types by at least 30%, collapsing the vocabulary toward a size consistent with a 3,000â€“5,000-root natural language
evidence_for: Word-final glyph set {y,n,l,r,o} is highly constrained and accounts for the overwhelming majority of word endings
Hapax ratio of 70.1% far exceeds Latin (~45%) or Italian (~50%), suggesting artificial inflation
Top frequent words like 'daiin','aiin','chedy','shedy' share common suffixes, consistent with a suffix paradigm
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is below 1.0, consistent with a vocabulary inflated by morphological variants rather than semantic diversity
evidence_against: If suffixes encode cipher structure rather than grammar, stripping them may not reduce vocabulary meaningfully
Currier A/B split could mean two different suffix systems, complicating normalization
Word entropy of 10.45 bits is high, suggesting genuine vocabulary diversity rather than paradigmatic expansion
test: Strip the final glyph from every word token in the full corpus (38,053 words) and recompute: unique word types, hapax count, and hapax ratio on the stemmed vocabulary. Compare stemmed Zipf fit (R-squared) against unstemmed.
test_metric: Reduction in unique word types (%), stemmed hapax ratio, stemmed Zipf R-squared
test_threshold: Confirmation: stemmed hapax ratio drops below 50% and stemmed Zipf R-squared rises above 0.95. Denial: stemmed hapax ratio remains above 65% or R-squared does not improve by >0.03.

tests_run.test: strip the final glyph from every word token in the full corpus (38,053 words) and recompute: unique
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If stripping a single suffix glyph substantially collapses the vocabulary and improves Zipf fit, it confirms that word-final positional glyphs are grammatical markers (inflection/case endings) rather than cipher artifacts, which would narrow decipherment to inflected natural languages and allow paradigm-table reconstruction.
